You are a famous and esteemed scientist and professor in health economics with a proven track record of publications in top academic journals such as Journal of Health Economics, Health Economics, Social Science & Medicine, Annals of Internal Medicine, Lancet, JAMA, and BMJ. Your writing has a scientific rigor that ensures clarity and precision in language. I will give you points that cover the main content of each section (i.e., introduction, methods, results, and discussion), and you will use the points to write a paper that will be submitted to JAMA Network Open. Please use the writing style that fits the target journal. The language should be grammatically correct, and that the ideas are presented in a clear and concise manner. Please ensure the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concision and overall readability. Please use appropriate and precise words and sentences should flow fluently. Now, I will feed you the points of my abstract , and please use them to generate the abstract section. Abstract
Importance: Familial hypercholesterolemia is a prevalent genetic disorder globally, significantly impacting severe cardiovascular diseases. As genetic disease screening and treatment advance, evaluating the economic implications of familial hypercholesterolemia becomes increasingly crucial. International disparities persist, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive economic analysis of familial hypercholesterolemia screening for valuable insights.

Objective: To conduct a global analysis of economic assessments pertaining to familial hypercholesterolemia, synthesize evidence, and offer economic insights into the impact of familial hypercholesterolemia screening on population equity.

Data Sources: A systematic search was conducted on familial hypercholesterolemia screening economic evaluation literature from 2000 to October 2023, utilizing PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. Inclusion and quality assessments employed the QHES and CHEERS 2022 instruments.

Study Selection: Out of 189 identified articles, 19 familial hypercholesterolemia screening economic evaluation studies were included.

Methods: A systematic literature analysis evaluated 19 familial hypercholesterolemia screening economic evaluation studies. Through meta-analysis, evidence was synthesized, comprehensively analyzing the total cost-effectiveness and total net health benefits of cascade and universal screening for familial hypercholesterolemia across various health outcomes. Aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis assessed FH screening's impact on population equity.

Results: Economic evaluations of familial hypercholesterolemia screening exhibited significant variations across countries. Cost-effectiveness analysis indicated that familial hypercholesterolemia screening is cost-effective in the majority of countries. Meta-analysis synthesized the economic impacts of seven sets of cascade and universal screening across diverse health outcomes. Aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis suggested the potential for familial hypercholesterolemia screening strategies to alleviate health inequalities.

Conclusions and Relevance: This study offers a global perspective on the economic evaluation of familial hypercholesterolemia screening. Despite international differences, the majority of cases suggest that familial hypercholesterolemia screening is cost-effective, contributing to improved health equity and overall population health. The optimistic outlook underscores the significance of familial hypercholesterolemia screening in shaping future health decisions.

Abstract

Importance: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is a widespread genetic disorder that substantially influences the incidence of severe cardiovascular diseases. In light of advancements in genetic screening and therapies, there is an escalating necessity to appraise the economic repercussions of FH. Inconsistencies on an international scale highlight the urgency for an exhaustive economic assessment of FH screening to procure valuable insights and enhance understanding.

Objective: This study aims to execute a global examination of economic assessments related to FH screening, to aggregate evidence, and to elucidate the economic connotations of FH screening with a focus on its effects on population-level health equity.

Methods: We performed a systematic literature review targeting economic evaluations of FH screening published from January 2000 to October 2023. Databases searched included PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. The Quality of Health Economic Studies (QHES) instrument and Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards (CHEERS) 2022 statement guided the inclusion and quality appraisal of the literature. Meta-analysis techniques were applied to integrate findings from various studies, focusing on the cost-effectiveness and net health benefits associated with both cascade and universal screening programs for FH. An aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis was employed to determine the potential impact of screening programs on health equity across populations.

Results: Nineteen economic evaluation studies were eligible for inclusion out of 189 screened articles. Our analysis revealed substantial international variability in the economic valuation of FH screening. Pooled findings from these studies suggest that FH screening tends to be cost-effective in a majority of the analyzed countries. Results from the meta-analysis highlighted economic impacts derived from seven distinct screening approaches, addressing a range of health outcomes. The aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis underscored the ability of FH screening protocols to mitigate health disparities.

Conclusions and Relevance: Our comprehensive global analysis indicates that FH screening is predominantly characterized as cost-effective across a diverse set of countries, leading to the optimistic inference that it presents an opportunity to enhance health equity and improve overall population health outcomes. These findings lend substantial support to the integration of FH screening into health policy frameworks to inform future health care decisions and prioritize strategies that align with evidence-based economic insights.

Methods: We performed a systematic literature review targeting economic evaluations of FH screening published from January 2000 to October 2023. Databases searched included PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. The Quality of Health Economic Studies instrument and Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards 2022 statement guided the inclusion and quality appraisal of the literature. Meta-analysis techniques were applied to integrate findings from various studies, focusing on the total cost-effectiveness and net health benefits associated with both cascade and universal screening programs for FH. An aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis was employed to determine the potential impact of screening programs on health equity across populations. 使简单明了

Methods 

In this study, we conducted a systematic review of the economic evaluations of FH screening. Our search spanned literature published from January 2000 through October 2023, encompassing studies indexed in PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. We rigorously assessed the quality and relevance of studies for inclusion using the Quality of Health Economic Studies (QHES) instrument alongside the 2022 Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards (CHEERS) checklist. 

Our analysis employed meta-analytic techniques to synthesize data from the selected studies, with an emphasis on assessing both the cost-effectiveness and the net health benefits of cascade and universal FH screening methods. Additionally, we implemented aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis to examine the effect of FH screening on health equity, with the aim of understanding its potential to reduce health disparities across differing population groups.

Methods: We performed a systematic literature review targeting economic evaluations of FH screening published from January 2000 to October 2023. Databases searched included PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. The Quality of Health Economic Studies instrument and Consolidated Health Economic Evaluation Reporting Standards 2022 statement guided the inclusion and quality appraisal of the literature. Meta-analysis techniques were applied to integrate findings from various studies, focusing on the total cost-effectiveness and net health benefits associated with both cascade and universal screening programs for FH. An aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis was employed to determine the potential impact of screening programs on health equity across populations. 使更简洁

Methods

We conducted a systematic literature review, sourcing economic evaluations of FH screening from January 2000 to October 2023. The search encompassed PubMed, Web of Science, and Embase. Literature inclusion and appraisal were governed by the Quality of Health Economic Studies instrument and CHEERS 2022 guidelines. We amalgamated study findings using meta-analysis, focusing on the cost-effectiveness and net health benefits of cascade and universal FH screening. Furthermore, we applied aggregate distributional cost-effectiveness analysis to assess the implications for health equity.

Our analysis revealed that there is considerable international variability in the economic evaluations of FH screening. The aggregated findings from the studies indicate that, in the majority of countries analyzed, FH screening is generally cost-effective.

Our analysis highlights notable international differences in FH screening's economic evaluations, with data synthesis indicating that FH screening is cost-effective in most analyzed countries.

Importance: Familial hypercholesterolemia is a widespread genetic disorder that substantially influences the incidence of severe cardiovascular diseases. In light of advancements in genetic screening and therapies, there is an escalating necessity to appraise the economic repercussions of familial hypercholesterolemia. International disparities persist, emphasizing the need for a comprehensive economic analysis of familial hypercholesterolemia screening for valuable insights.两句话概括

Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) significantly impacts cardiovascular disease risk and, with medical advancements, assessing its economic burden becomes critical. Global disparities in FH highlight the urgency for a thorough economic analysis of its screening programs.
